title: Roth vs Traditional IRA: Complete Decision Guide
description: Comprehensive comparison of Roth vs Traditional IRAs. Learn about contribution limits, tax benefits, and how to choose the right account for retirement.

mainEntity.name: Should I choose a Roth or Traditional IRA in 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: The choice between a Roth and Traditional IRA depends primarily on your current tax bracket versus your expected tax bracket in retirement. If you expect your tax rate to be higher in retirement, a Roth IRA is generally better. If you expect it to be lower, a Traditional IRA may offer better upfront tax savings.

mainEntity.name: What are the IRA contribution limits for 2024, 2025, and 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: For 2024 and 2025, the contribution limit for both Roth and Traditional IRAs is $7,000, with an additional $1,000 catch-up contribution for those age 50 or older. Limits for 2026 have not yet been officially announced by the IRS.

mainEntity.name: What are the income phase-outs for a Roth IRA?

acceptedAnswer.text: For 2024, the income phase-out range for single filers is $146,000 to $161,000, and for married filing jointly, it is $230,000 to $240,000. For 2025, the ranges increased to $150,000-$165,000 for singles and $236,000-$246,000 for married couples.

mainEntity.name: Can I convert a Traditional IRA to a Roth IRA?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, you can convert funds from a Traditional IRA to a Roth IRA at any time. This is often called a Roth conversion. However, you will have to pay ordinary income tax on the pre-tax amount you convert in the year of the conversion.

mainEntity.name: What are the RMD rules for Roth vs Traditional IRAs?

acceptedAnswer.text: Traditional IRAs are subject to Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs) starting at age 73 (or 75 for those born in 1960 or later). Roth IRAs do not have RMDs during the original owner's lifetime.
